perturbation.original_text: Dealer shall not distribute, disseminate, publish or disclose the Confidential Information to any person, or entity not employed or retained by Dealer or the use of the Confidential Information for any purpose other than to further the marketing and sale of CIF products.
perturbation.changed_text: Dealer shall endeavor to avoid distributing, disseminating, publishing or disclosing the Confidential Information to any person or entity not employed or retained by Dealer or the use of the Confidential Information for purposes unrelated to the general promotion of CIF.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'shall not' to 'shall endeavor to avoid' weakens the obligation. Additionally, 'general promotion of CIF' is less precise than 'further the marketing and sale of CIF products,' making the scope of permitted use ambiguous. This creates an ambiguous obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA)
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA requires organizations to protect personal information. By weakening the non-disclosure obligation, the modification increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure of personal information, potentially violating PIPEDA. Specifically, Principle 4.5 states that personal information shall be protected by security safeguards appropriate to the sensitivity of the information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act Skip to main content Skip to "About this site" Skip to section menu PART 1 Protection of Personal Information in the Private Sector (continued) DIVISION 4 General (continued) Marginal note: Promoting the purposes of the Part 24 Â The Commissioner shall (a) Â develop and conduct information programs to foster public understanding, and recognition of the purposes, of this Part; (b) Â undertake and publish research that is related to the protection of personal information, including any such research that is requested by the Minister of Industry; (c) Â encourage organizations to develop detailed policies and practices, including organizational codes of practice, to comply with Divisions 1 and 1.1; and (d) Â promote, by any means that the Commissioner considers appropriate, the purposes of this Part. 2000, c. 5, s. 24 2015, c. 32, s. 19 Previous Version Marginal note: Annual report 25 Â (1) Â The Commissioner shall, within three months after the end of each financial year, submit to Parliament a report concerning the application of this Part, the extent to which the provinces have enacted legislation that is substantially similar to this Part and the application of any such legislation. Marginal note: Consultation (2) Â Before preparing the report, the Commissioner shall consult with those persons in the provinces who, in the Commissionerâs opinion, are in a position to assist the Commissioner in making a report respecting personal information that is collected, used or disclosed interprovincially or internationally. 2000, c. 5, s. 25 2015, c. 32, s. 20 Previous Version Marginal note: Regulations 26 Â (1) Â The Governor in Council may make regulations for carrying out the purposes and provisions of this Part, including regulations (a) Â specifying, by name or by class, what is a government institution or part of a government institution for the purposes of any provision of this Part; (a.01) Â [Repealed, 2015, c. 32, s. 21] (a.1) Â specifying information or classes of information for the purpose of paragraphÂ 7(1)(d), (2)(c.1) or (3)(h.1); (b) Â specifying information to be kept and maintained under subsectionÂ 10.3(1); and (c) Â prescribing anything that by this Part is to be prescribed. Marginal note: Orders (2) Â The Governor in Council may, by order, (a) Â provide that this Part is binding on any agent of Her Majesty in right of Canada to which the Privacy Act does not apply; (b) Â if satisfied that legislation of a province that is substantially similar to this Part applies to an organization, a class of organizations, an activity or a class of activities, exempt the organization, activity or class from the application of this Part in respect of the collection, use or disclosure of personal information that occurs within that province; and (c) Â amend ScheduleÂ 4. 2000, c. 5, s. 26 2015, c. 32, s. 21, c. 36, s. 165 Previous Version Marginal note: Whistleblowing 27 Â (1) Â Any person who has reasonable grounds to believe that a person has contravened or intends to contravene a provision of Division 1 or 1.1 may notify the Commissioner of the particulars of the matter and may request that their identity be kept confidential with respect to the notification. Marginal note: Confidentiality (2) Â The Commissioner shall keep confidential the identity of a person who has notified the Commissioner under subsection (1) and to whom an assurance of confidentiality has been provided by the Commissioner. 2000, c. 5, s. 27 2015, c. 32, s. 22 Previous Version Marginal note: Prohibition 27.1 Â (1) Â No employer shall dismiss, suspend, demote, discipline, harass or otherwise disadvantage an employee, or deny an employee a benefit of employment, by reason that (a) Â the employee, acting in good faith and on the basis of reasonable belief, has disclosed to the Commissioner that the employer or any other person has contravened or intends to contravene a provision of Division 1 or 1.1;
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates that the dealer 'shall not' disclose confidential information, whereas the changed text states that the dealer 'shall endeavor to avoid' disclosing this information. This introduces ambiguity and weakens the legal obligation to protect personal information, as the term 'endeavor to avoid' does not provide a clear and enforceable standard. The Law Explanation stated that 'PIPEDA requires organizations to protect personal information. By weakening the non-disclosure obligation, the modification increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure of personal information, potentially violating PIPEDA.' The scraped snippet 2 generally references PIPEDA's aims for information protection, and while it doesn't provide a specific clause that is violated, it does highlight the importance of information protection, which is undermined by the contract modification.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that CIF requests that Dealer return any of its Confidential Information, Dealer agrees to return all of the Confidential Information delivered to it, and each copy or duplicate thereof, within thirty (30) days of the date of the request thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that CIF requests that Dealer return any of its Confidential Information, Dealer should make a reasonable effort to return the Confidential Information within a timeframe it deems appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'agrees to return all' with 'should make a reasonable effort to return' and specifying 'within thirty (30) days' with 'a timeframe it deems appropriate' introduces significant ambiguity. The phrase 'reasonable effort' is vague and does not guarantee the return of confidential information. The thirty-day clause is also changed to let the Dealer decide on the return time.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA)
perturbation.law_explanation: Failing to ensure the return of confidential information upon request undermines the security safeguards necessary to protect personal information under PIPEDA, specifically Principle 4.5.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates a clear obligation for the Dealer to return confidential information within a specific timeframe (30 days). The changed text significantly weakens this obligation by stating the Dealer 'should make a reasonable effort' and can return the information within 'a timeframe it deems appropriate.' This introduces ambiguity and discretion where there was none, potentially undermining the protection of confidential information. The LLM explanation points to a failure to ensure the return of confidential information upon request, which it argues undermines the security safeguards necessary to protect personal information under PIPEDA. While the provided snippet doesn't explicitly outline the return of confidential information upon request under PIPEDA, the change from 'agrees to return all' to 'should make a reasonable effort' creates a significant ambiguity around the enforceability of the obligation to return confidential information in a timely fashion.

perturbation.original_text: Any notice required or permitted u nder this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be delivered and deemed received as follows: (i) by personal delivery or by facsimile (with electronic confirmation) during the recipient’s normal business hours, in which case it shall be deemed received at the time of such delivery or facsimile as the case may be; (ii) by certified or registered mail, in which case it shall be deemed received on the date such return receipt is signed; or (iii) by prepaid nationally recognized overnight courier by next day delivery, in which case it shall be deemed received on the day following the date of deposit with such courier.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice related to this Agreement may be provided through reasonable means, such as email or verbal communication, at the discretion of the sending party.
perturbation.explanation: This change completely removes the structured, defined methods of notice with specific conditions for receipt (personal delivery, fax, certified mail, courier) and replaces it with a vague 'reasonable means' which could include email or verbal communication, which are less reliable and lack defined receipt confirmation. This creates ambiguity as to what constitutes proper notice.
perturbation.law_citation: Electronic Commerce Act, 2000, S.O. 2000, c. 17, s. 22
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 22 of Ontario's Electronic Commerce Act deals with the sending and receipt of electronic documents, which may apply here given the context of electronic information. By making acceptable notice methods vague, the changes undermine the legal certainty afforded by the Electronic Commerce Act, which establishes rules for when electronic documents are considered sent and received.

Preservation of other laws re privacy, access to information 28. Legal requirements to which Act does not a
perturbation.justification: The original contract text specifies precise methods for delivering notices, ensuring clarity and verifiability. The changed text introduces ambiguity by allowing 'reasonable means' such as email or verbal communication, which are less reliable and lack defined receipt confirmation. The Electronic Commerce Act, 2000, S.O. 2000, c. 17, addresses the sending and receipt of electronic documents. By making notice methods vague, the change undermines the legal certainty the Act intends to provide, especially regarding when electronic documents are considered sent and received, creating a contradiction as the altered clause weakens the ability to prove notice compared to the original clause.

perturbation.original_text: Should any legal proceedings be commenced to secure or enforce any right under this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover from the non-prevailing party its reasonable attorneys' fees and costs, in addition to all other relief to which the prevailing party may be entitled at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of legal proceedings related to this Agreement, the court may consider awarding attorney's fees to the prevailing party based on its assessment of fairness and equity.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states the prevailing party 'shall be entitled' to recover attorney's fees. The modified text makes this discretionary ('the court may consider') and introduces subjective criteria ('assessment of fairness and equity'). This creates ambiguity and weakens the right to recover attorney's fees.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, Rule 57.01
perturbation.law_citation: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, Rule 57.01
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 57.01 outlines the factors a court can consider when determining costs. While it doesn't mandate attorney's fees in every case, the original clause in the contract provides a stronger entitlement than the modified clause. Making it subject to the court's 'assessment of fairness and equity' introduces uncertainty that could undermine a party's legitimate claim for costs, going against the spirit of predictable cost recovery in litigation.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates attorney's fees for the prevailing party, while the changed text makes it discretionary ('may consider') based on 'fairness and equity.' This introduces ambiguity where there was none, weakening the right to recover attorney's fees. Scraped Law Snippet 1, RULE 57 COSTS OF PROCEEDINGS 57.01 General Principles, relates to general principles of cost awards in proceedings. While it does not explicitly mandate attorney fees, the original contract clause provides a stronger guarantee of cost recovery than the modified version which makes it subject to a court's assessment of fairness and equity. This change undermines the predictable cost recovery implied in the original text.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement, and all questions relating to its validity, interpretation, performance, and enforcement (including, without limitation, provisions concerning limitations of action) shall be go verned by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Province of Ontario, Canada.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement, and all questions relating to its general principles, may be interpreted under principles of law considered suitable by both parties involved.
perturbation.explanation: The original specifies Ontario law governs interpretation and enforcement. The modified text replaces this clear choice of law with a vague reference to 'principles of law considered suitable by both parties.' This introduces ambiguity by not specifying a jurisdiction or body of law, opening the door to disputes about which legal principles apply. This directly negates the governing law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ontario's Choice of Law Rules (Common Law Principles)
perturbation.law_citation: Common Law Principles - Choice of Law
perturbation.law_explanation: While there isn't a single statute codifying choice of law rules in Ontario for contracts, common law principles dictate that a clear choice of law provision (as in the original text) is generally upheld. By removing the reference to Ontario law and replacing it with a vague agreement on 'suitable' principles, the modified text undermines the established legal framework for determining which law governs the contract, potentially leading to significant legal uncertainty.

perturbation.justification: The original contract clearly specifies Ontario law for governing validity, interpretation, performance, and enforcement. The changed text replaces this with a vague reference to 'principles of law considered suitable by both parties involved', introducing ambiguity about which legal principles apply. This ambiguity directly undermines the governing law, opening the door to disputes and legal uncertainty. The Uniform Law Commission snippet, while not directly on point, highlights the importance of clarity and stability in state statutory law, which is undermined by the change. The absence of a clear governing law creates a situation where the contract's interpretation and enforcement become unpredictable, which is a direct contradiction of the original intent and established legal practices.
